ScopeDocketShape.description:
A docket-number *shape* and how many unparseable open events carry it.

The shape masks digits→``9`` and letters→``A``/``a`` (punctuation/space kept), so
``01-7700`` → ``99-9999`` and ``22O141`` → ``99A999`` — every uppercase letter
masks to ``A``, so a shape names a format class, not a specific docket letter.
It tells us, concretely, which docket formats drive the "Term not parseable"
bucket — i.e. exactly what the Term parser would need to handle to bring those
events into scope. A shape carrying fewer than ~100 open events is an accepted
fragment: it stays visible here by design, and no exclusion predicate is added
for it.

count.description: Unparseable open events with this shape
count.title: Count

ScopeDocketShape.title: ScopeDocketShape

ScopeExclusion.description: One exclusion predicate's footprint among the corpus's open events.

recoverable.description: Of those open events, how many sit on a case carrying an opinion / citation / decision-date signal — a hint the disposition may be recoverable (an ingestion gap) rather than genuinely absent
recoverable.title: Recoverable

sample_cases.description: A bounded sample of matched case ids, for triage
sample_cases.title: Sample Cases

ScopeExclusion.title: ScopeExclusion

ScopeUnclassified.description:
Why an open SCOTUS event the scope did *not* exclude stays in scope (refinement signal).

The refinement surface: buckets the open events no predicate caught by the reason
each is still in scope — a recent/current Term (legitimately pending), a docket
Term the parser cannot read (a format the predicate skips → a broadening
candidate), a recorded disposition the event-state missed, or no docket number.

reason.description: Why this open event was not excluded
reason.title: Reason

ScopeUnclassified.title: ScopeUnclassified

description:
``corpus-scope-audit`` verdict: open events the predict scope excludes.

A read-only census of the corpus's still-**open** events whose case an exclusion
predicate drops at the matrix gate (pre-1925 mandatory jurisdiction, stale
unresolvable old SCOTUS petitions). These sit open in the corpus forever
because nothing resolves them, so they are the candidates for the corpus-side
corpus reconcile (resolve if recoverable, else latch out of scope). The
``recoverable`` split is what tells those two paths apart. ``skipped`` is set
(with empty exclusions) when the corpus is absent, so it is safe before a
``dvc pull``. A pure function of the corpus, so it carries no timestamp.

exclusions.description: One entry per exclusion predicate that matched
exclusions.title: Exclusions

scotus_open_events.description: Total open (unresolved) SCOTUS events — the audit's denominator
scotus_open_events.title: Scotus Open Events

skipped.description: True when no corpus was present; nothing was audited
skipped.title: Skipped

unclassified.description: Open SCOTUS events no predicate excluded, bucketed by why
unclassified.title: Unclassified

unparseable_docket_shapes.description: Top docket-number shapes in the 'Term not parseable' bucket — the concrete formats a parser broadening would target; shapes under ~100 open events are accepted fragments, left visible here by design with no predicate chased for them
unparseable_docket_shapes.title: Unparseable Docket Shapes

title: CorpusScopeAudit
